title: Rootless exec fails, unable to write to cgroup.procs
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

When running a rootless container I'm unable to `podman exec` in to that container.

For example:

```
$ podman  run --rm  -it docker.io/ubuntu bash
root@e37dd236bf3a:/#
```

then in another terminal:

```
$ podman exec e37dd236bf3a id
Error: crun: writing file `/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/user.slice/libpod-e37dd236bf3ad816e50f82c8fa6204b18322aa924e3e46e590154abd69fc8adb.scope/container/cgroup.procs`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied
```

It does work though if I do:

```
$ systemd-run --user --scope podman exec e37dd236bf3a id
Running scope as unit: run-ra349205238984ea59d71e8f96729ede2.scope
uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root)
```

Looking at the cgroup info I find:

```
$ cat /proc/self/cgroup
0::/user.slice/user-1000.slice/session-1.scope

$ cat /proc/BASH-PID/cgroup
0::/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/user.slice/libpod-e37dd236bf3ad816e50f82c8fa6204b18322aa924e3e46e590154abd69fc8adb.scope/container
```

If I look in `/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice` for `cgroup.procs` files I see that root owns the one in that directory, but the ones under libpod and my session are owned by me.

Looking at an `strace` for the read:

```
236520 openat(AT_FDCWD, "/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/user.slice/libpod-e37dd236bf3ad816e50f82c8fa6204b18322aa924e3e46e590154abd69fc8adb.scope/container/cgroup.procs", O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC, 0700) = 8
236520 write(8, "236524", 6)            = -1 EACCES (Permission denied)
```

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.1.1
API Version:  4.1.1
Go Version:   go1.17.11
Built:        Mon Dec 31 20:00:00 1979
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

NixOS, physical

comments.body: this exact process works for me. I have recently rehauled cgroup handling in podman, some of which might be in 4.1.1 and some of which is not. Can you try running this off of the main branch and see if the issue goes away?
alright I will look at this to see If i can reproduce. @giuseppe thoughts on this one?
I tried to reproduce on f35, no luck. I have no access to Nix, or even any idea what Nix is.

Okay, so for some testing I started an f36 vm. It also doesn't happen by default so I started comparing differences. It looks like it happens if I set the owner on the session scope:

`chown kenny:kenny /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/session-1.scope`

Now sure why they're different though, or why it matters.

what is the current cgroup (`cat /proc/self/cgroup`)?

If it is not owned by your user, then the kernel blocks moving the process out of it
@giuseppe Just a normal host shell? On Nix it's owned by me. On the Fedora VM it's owned by root.  Oddly it's when it's owned by me that it doesn't work.
but what is the cgroup in the "normal host shell"?  Does it work better if you do something like `systemd-run --scope --user podman exec e37dd236bf3a id` ?
@giuseppe The group is `0::/user.slice/user-1000.slice/session-1.scope` and running with `systemd-run --scope --user` does make it work.

isn't `/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/session-1.scope` owned by root?  At least on my system it is:

```
$ ls -ld /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/session-*.scope
drwxr-xr-x. 2 root root 0 Jun 23 15:17 /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/session-3.scope
```

@giuseppe Nope, that's part of the issue. On Fedora it's owned by root, on NixOS (at least mine) it's owned by the user.

`$ ls /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/session-1.scope -al`
```ls
drwxr-xr-x kenny users 0  Fri Jul  8 14:48:10 2022 .
drwxr-xr-x root  root  0  Fri Jul  8 14:47:50 2022 ..
.r--r--r-- root  root  0  Fri Jul  8 14:59:02 2022 cgroup.controllers
```

From the `cgroups(7)` man page:
```
      Some delegation containment rules ensure that the delegatee can move processes between cgroups within the delegated subtree, but can't move processes from outside the delegated subtree into  the  sub‐
       tree or vice versa.  A nonprivileged process (i.e., the delegatee) can write the PID of a "target" process into a cgroup.procs file only if all of the following are true:

       *  The writer has write permission on the cgroup.procs file in the destination cgroup.

       *  The  writer  has  write  permission  on  the cgroup.procs file in the nearest common ancestor of the source and destination cgroups.  Note that in some cases, the nearest common ancestor may be the
          source or destination cgroup itself.  This requirement is not enforced for cgroups v1 hierarchies, with the consequence that containment in v1 is less strict than in v2.  (For example,  in  cgroups
          v1 the user that owns two distinct delegated subhierarchies can move a process between the hierarchies.)

       *  If the cgroup v2 filesystem was mounted with the nsdelegate option, the writer must be able to see the source and destination cgroups from its cgroup namespace.

       *  In  cgroups  v1:  the effective UID of the writer (i.e., the delegatee) matches the real user ID or the saved set-user-ID of the target process.  Before Linux 4.11, this requirement also applied in
          cgroups v2 (This was a historical requirement inherited from cgroups v1 that was later deemed unnecessary, since the other rules suffice for containment in cgroups v2.)
```

I think your configuration doesn't still honor the second rule for moving a process to a different cgroup as you'd have to own the `user-1000.slice` cgroup.

I am closing the issue but please keep adding more comments.  Also reopen if I am wrong and you still think it is an issue in Podman itself :-)

Thanks @giuseppe! I did some more digging in to permissions and the like. I found that if I have `lxcfs` installed I no longer end up with the `nsdelegate` option on the `cgroup2` mount. Removing `lxcfs` (I wasn't actually using it currently) makes everything work as expected.

Hopefully this helps others. It sounds like it's not a Podman bug, but maybe it's worth including the `nsdelegate` requirement in the rootless docs? (or the mount options in the `podman info --debug` output?)

I seem to be running into the same issue on Fedora 36 with podman 4.2.0. I can reproduce in the same manner as listed on this ticket. `podman  run --rm  -it docker.io/ubuntu bash` followed by `podman exec 36fd7d5165cc id` in another terminal leads to:
```
Error: crun: writing file `/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/user.slice/libpod-36fd7d5165cc2cba2b9fcddc37ae9f60d2e75424315ce8b1f007d10dc0ca890a.scope/container/cgroup.procs`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied
```

I checked that session is owned by root:
```
$ ls -ld /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/session-*.scope
drwxr-xr-x. 2 root root 0 Oct 16 17:41 /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice/session-2.scope
```
And that `nsdelegate` is enabled for `cgroup2`:
```
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot)
```

@giuseppe you mentioned that you thought the kernel would block if the cgroup is not owned by the user. You also mentioned that root owns the cgroup on your system (just like mine). Any ideas of what I might be doing wrong?
what is the current cgroup where podman is running in?  You can check it with `cat /proc/self/cgroup`
If someone else still suffers from this issue: the solution for me (Debian 10) was to install dbus-user-session.

@grzs We get the same error with github runners (ubuntu) where dbus-user-session is already installed. If anyone has other ideas... 


Podman reports 3.4.4
